rationale_for_baseline_tier: The presentation is a classic benign kinetosis (severe motion sickness) picture with stable vitals, an alert patient, no focal neurological deficits and an overwhelmingly clear kinetic trigger, so an ambulance (T5) or emergency department (T4) response would be over-triage in the absence of stroke red flags. However, pure self-care (T0/T1) is unsafe here for two reasons that meet the narrow telehealth uplift: first, a posterior-circulation (cerebellar) stroke can present identically and can only be excluded by an in-person focused neurological examination that a telehealth service cannot perform; second, the patient has intractable vomiting with a total inability to tolerate oral fluids and lives alone, so a retainable (sublingual/parenteral) antiemetic and hydration assessment are needed in person. The correct disposition is therefore urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3) — urgent care, an after-hours home visit, or a GP able to examine — alongside immediately-actionable symptom and sipping-rehydration advice.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any focal neurological feature — sudden severe headache, one-sided weakness or numbness, slurred or difficult speech, double vision, or worsening unsteadiness out of proportion to the vertigo
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of a posterior-circulation (cerebellar/brainstem) stroke, a time-critical emergency where a benign vestibular cause can no longer be assumed.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 immediately if you get a sudden severe headache, weakness or numbness on one side, slurred speech, double vision, or you become much more unsteady than the dizziness alone would explain.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Signs of significant dehydration — still unable to keep any fluid down, no urine passed for more than 12 hours, marked light-headedness on sitting up, or new confusion
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day / within hours if it develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent inability to retain fluids with reduced urine output indicates clinical dehydration needing intravenous rehydration and in-person care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Go to the emergency department the same day if you still cannot keep any fluid down, you have not passed urine for more than 12 hours, or you feel very faint when you sit up.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting blood or material that looks like coffee grounds
escalation_edges.rationale: Blood in the vomit after repeated forceful retching suggests an oesophagogastric tear or upper-GI bleed requiring urgent in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Get emergency help straight away if you vomit blood or something that looks like dark coffee grounds.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe vertigo and vomiting persisting beyond 24 hours despite vestibular rest, or not settling after the same-day assessment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: GP review within 24-48 hours if not settling
escalation_edges.rationale: Symptoms that outlast the expected course of motion sickness raise a peripheral vestibular cause (vestibular neuritis/labyrinthitis) and warrant reassessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the spinning and vomiting are still severe after a day of resting quietly, or they are not settling after you have been seen, book a GP review to reassess.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one at home to help or to notice deterioration, while unable to keep fluids down
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Prioritise a disposition that brings care to the patient or brings the patient safely to care (after-hours home visit or urgent care rather than expecting them to self-transport while vertiginous), give an explicit written-style list of red flags, and confirm the patient can summon help, since there is no one else present to escalate on their behalf.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a severe bout of seasickness (motion sickness), which is not dangerous in itself — but because you cannot keep any fluids down and because severe dizziness like this occasionally comes from a problem in the brain rather than the inner ear, you should be examined in person today (urgent care, an after-hours home-visit doctor, or a GP who can see you). In the meantime lie flat and keep your head still, and try one teaspoon of electrolyte fluid every 5 minutes. Do not drive yourself while you are this dizzy.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day in-person assessment — after-hours home-visit service, urgent care, or a GP able to examine; call 000 if a red flag develops
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours home-visit or urgent-care service for the same-day examination; call 000 immediately for any stroke red flag (sudden severe headache, one-sided weakness/numbness, slurred speech, double vision) and go to the emergency department if you cannot keep any fluid down or pass no urine for over 12 hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Acute severe kinetosis after a turbulent boat trip with intractable vomiting and inability to tolerate oral fluids; no focal neurological symptoms on history; central cause not excludable via telehealth. Referred for urgent same-day in-person focused neurological examination, retainable antiemetic and hydration assessment. GP to reassess and consider vestibular neuritis if symptoms persist beyond 24 hours.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Confirming benign motion sickness remotely and advising pure self-care with no in-person examination and no red-flag safety-netting.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A posterior-circulation (cerebellar) stroke masquerading as severe motion sickness is missed because the neurological examination that would exclude it was never arranged, and progressive dehydration goes unassessed in a patient living alone.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring to a routine GP review in 24-48 hours despite intractable vomiting, inability to tolerate any oral fluids, and an unexcludable central cause — the patient continues to dehydrate and a central cause remains unexamined for a further day or more, risking an avoidable emergency admission or a missed stroke.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a stable, alert patient with no focal deficits and an overwhelmingly clear kinetic trigger to the emergency department or calling an ambulance — unnecessary ambulance/ED use, cost and anxiety, and diversion of emergency resources, for a benign condition manageable with a same-day in-person assessment.
